name: Frida (Dynamic Instrumentation Toolkit)
pricing_notes: Open source (wxWindows Library Licence); Ole André Vadla Ravnås

auth.notes: Requires device access; Android debug bridge or jailbroken iOS

performance.notes: Instrumentation overhead; GumJS JIT compilation; near-native hooks

agent_readiness.notes: Dynamic binary instrumentation for reverse engineering and security research. import frida. session = frida.attach('process_name'). script = session.create_script(js_code). script.on('message', on_message). script.load(). JavaScript hooks in GumJS: Interceptor.attach(Module.getExportByName('lib', 'func'), { onEnter, onLeave }). Works on Android, iOS (jailbroken), Linux, macOS, Windows. USB device support for mobile. Objection built on Frida for mobile pentesting. Anti-detection cat-and-mouse game with anti-frida techniques.

gotchas: Target applications may have anti-Frida detection
iOS requires jailbreak or ipa-signed build
JavaScript scripting required for hooks (not pure Python)
Root/debug access required for most use cases
best_when: Mobile app security testing (Android/iOS dynamic analysis)
Runtime function hooking and argument inspection
Bypassing certificate pinning in mobile apps
Dynamic malware analysis and behavior monitoring
avoid_when: Static binary analysis (use Ghidra, angr, or radare2)
Non-security use cases (dynamic tracing tools like bpftrace better)
summary: Frida is a dynamic instrumentation toolkit for runtime binary analysis. frida.attach('process').create_script(js).load() — hooks functions via JavaScript GumJS. Interceptor.attach() for entry/exit hooks. Android/iOS (jailbroken)/Linux/macOS/Windows. Mobile SSL pinning bypass via Objection (Frida-based). Anti-Frida detection is a challenge. Standard tool for mobile app pentesting and runtime malware analysis.
